analysis_summary: The proposed method produced 8 finding(s) from 9 analysis obligation(s).

findings.summary: AES wrapper exposes live AES key material and internal intermediate state through software-visible MMIO read mux without a local privilege/debug/lock guard.
findings.vulnerability_category: read/expose / key_in/key_big and inter_state in aes_wrapper / permission-related RTL security issue
findings.reasoning_summary: Subject: software-visible AXI/APB/MMIO requester reaching AES through ariane_peripherals.aes -> axi2apb_64_32 -> apb_to_reg -> aes_wrapper.external_bus_io Operation: read/expose Object: key_in/key_big and inter_state in aes_wrapper Expected guard: reads of AES key material and internal intermediate state should be blocked or require an explicit privileged/debug-authorized mode; normal MMIO/APB reads should expose only non-secret status/ciphertext as intended Observed behavior: aes_wrapper drives external_bus_io.rdata with key_big slices and inter_state slices based only on external_bus_io.addr[6:2]. key_big is directly assigned from key_in. external_bus_io.ready is tied high and error tied low. No local privilege, authorization, debug, lock, or read-disable guard is present in the wrapper read mux. ariane_peripherals connects an AXI AES port through axi2apb and apb_to_reg into this REG_BUS interface. Path: AXI master -> axi_node_intf_wrap master[ariane_soc::AES] -> ariane_peripherals.aes AXI port -> i_axi2apb_64_32_aes -> i_apb_to_reg_aes -> reg_bus_aes -> aes_wrapper.external_bus_io -> read mux -> aes_prdata/aes.r_data Closure verdict: All required core evidence slots are present for a candidate violation.
findings.security_impact: A software-visible MMIO requester that can reach the AES window can disclose AES key material and internal AES intermediate state.

findings.summary: AES configuration writes are locally guarded only by write strobe and address decode; no local privilege/lock/busy guard is evident, but intended write-access policy is not established by the supplied evidence.
findings.vulnerability_category: write/configure/trigger / AES plaintext/control/state registers p_c, state, start / permission-related RTL security issue
findings.reasoning_summary: Subject: software-visible AXI/APB/MMIO requester reaching AES Operation: write/configure/trigger Object: AES plaintext/control/state registers p_c, state, start Expected guard: writes that configure cryptographic operation inputs/control should be constrained by valid address decode and any required privilege/lock policy for the AES peripheral; write strobes should not permit unintended update aliases Observed behavior: On reset aes_wrapper clears start, p_c, and state. After reset, if external_bus_io.write is asserted, addr[6:2] case values update start, p_c[3:0], and state[3:0]. There is address decode, but no local privilege/authorization check, no lock guard, and no busy/active-operation guard in the visible wrapper. Upstream fabric has a privilege connectivity map sourced from ROM2 access-control registers, but the intended policy for AES configuration writes and its complete enforcement cannot be concluded solely from the provided observations. Path: AXI master -> axi_node_intf_wrap -> ariane_peripherals.aes -> axi2apb_64_32_aes -> apb_to_reg_aes -> aes_wrapper.external_bus_io.write -> p_c/state/start -> aes_192_sed Closure verdict: Related RTL evidence exists, but some core evidence slots remain incomplete.
findings.security_impact: If AES operation control or plaintext/state programming is intended to be privileged, locked, or frozen while active, the local wrapper does not enforce that policy; however upstream access-control may mediate window reachability.
findings.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: Need full intended policy for AES configuration writes and complete proof that upstream access-control denies unauthorized writers.

findings.summary: bootrom is locally writable through req_i && we_i even though it is expected to be read-only ROM.
findings.vulnerability_category: read/write / bootrom mem contents exposed through rdata_o / permission-related RTL security issue
findings.reasoning_summary: Subject: AXI requester connected through testharness boot ROM path Operation: read/write Object: bootrom mem contents exposed through rdata_o Expected guard: boot ROM contents should be read-only; write requests should be ignored or rejected, and read access should be limited to intended boot address range/requesters by upstream decode or local guard Observed behavior: bootrom captures raddr_q on req_i && !we_i and writes mem[addr-derived index] <= wdata_i on req_i && we_i. rdata_o is continuously assigned from mem[raddr_q] when in range, otherwise zero. The testharness connects axi2mem outputs rom_req, rom_we, rom_addr, rom_wdata directly to bootrom ports. Path: AXI master -> axi_node_intf_wrap master[ariane_soc::ROM] -> axi2mem i_axi2rom -> rom_req/rom_we/rom_addr/rom_wdata -> bootrom.req_i/we_i/addr_i/wdata_i -> mem/rdata_o Closure verdict: All required core evidence slots are present for a candidate violation.
findings.security_impact: A requester able to issue writes to the ROM window can modify boot ROM contents and potentially alter boot code or security initialization.

findings.summary: ROM2/FUSE contents containing AES/JTAG/access-control key material are readable and writable through an AXI-visible ROM2_FUSE path, and writes can alter secure_reg outputs used for jtag_key and access-control registers.
findings.vulnerability_category: read/write/expose / rom2 secure_reg contents, AES/JTAG/access-control key material, and key-location/access-control outputs / permission-related RTL security issue
findings.reasoning_summary: Subject: software-visible AXI/MMIO requester in ariane_peripherals through ROM2_FUSE window Operation: read/write/expose Object: rom2 secure_reg contents, AES/JTAG/access-control key material, and key-location/access-control outputs Expected guard: ROM2 contents and any key-location output should be read-only or access-controlled; writes should be rejected/ignored and reads of key-related locations should require intended privilege/authorization or be non-secret by design Observed behavior: rom2 initializes secure_reg from const mem on reset. If req_i is asserted and we_i is 0, it captures raddr_q; if req_i and we_i are 1, it writes secure_reg[index] <= wdata_i. rdata_o returns secure_reg[raddr_q] when in range. ariane_peripherals connects ROM2 through axi2mem to the rom2_fuse AXI port and derives jtag_key and access_ctrl_reg from key_reg_out/secure_reg. No local privilege or write-protect guard is visible in rom2. Path: AXI requester -> ariane_peripherals.rom2_fuse AXI port -> axi2mem i_axi2rom2 -> rom2_req/rom2_we/rom2_addr/rom2_wdata -> rom2.secure_reg/rdata_o -> rom2_rdata plus key_reg_out -> jtag_key/access_ctrl_reg Closure verdict: All required core evidence slots are present for a candidate violation.
findings.security_impact: An MMIO requester reaching ROM2 can read key material and can modify jtag_key/access-control policy state, potentially unlocking debug or changing fabric permissions.

findings.summary: Debug/JTAG/DMI path reaches core debug control, debug reset, and an AXI debug master without visible authentication or jtag_key authorization gating in the supplied RTL observations.
findings.vulnerability_category: read/write/control/reset / SoC memory/peripherals/core debug control reachable via debug_req/debug_resp and AXI debug master/slave / permission-related RTL security issue
findings.reasoning_summary: Subject: JTAG/DMI debug requester Operation: read/write/control/reset Object: SoC memory/peripherals/core debug control reachable via debug_req/debug_resp and AXI debug master/slave Expected guard: JTAG/DMI debug operations that can read/write memory or control the core should require debug authorization/authentication or an explicit test/debug enable lifecycle state, and must not bypass normal privilege/PMP for protected regions unless authorized Observed behavior: ariane_testharness instantiates SimJTAG, dmi_jtag, SimDTM, and dm_top. dm_top outputs ndmreset and debug_req_core, accepts DMI requests through debug_req_valid/debug_req, and has AXI master/slave connections. debug_req_core is wired directly to the core. dm_axi_m_req is connected through axi_master_connect to slave[1], giving the debug module an AXI master path into the fabric. test_en is tied to 1'b0, but no visible comparison against jtag_key or authentication/lifecycle authorization is shown in the provided evidence. jtag_key is declared and produced from ROM2, but no use-site gating debug requests is evidenced. Path: SimJTAG/dmi_jtag/SimDTM -> debug_req/debug_resp DMI signals -> dm_top -> debug_req_core/ndmreset and dm_axi_m_req/dm_axi_m_resp -> axi_master_connect -> slave[1] -> axi_node_intf_wrap -> SoC memory/peripherals Closure verdict: All required core evidence slots are present for a candidate violation.
findings.security_impact: Unauthenticated debug access can halt/control the core, reset the system, and read/write SoC address spaces through the debug AXI master if the debug path is externally reachable in the target configuration.
findings.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: Full internals of SimJTAG/dmi_jtag/SimDTM/dm_top and any possible external debug authentication are not included. Candidate is based on absence of visible authorization at harness integration.

findings.summary: AXI wrapper includes a privilege/access-control connectivity map and passes it to axi_node, but complete denial behavior and correctness for read/write protected routing cannot be established from supplied observations.
findings.vulnerability_category: read/write/route / AXI subordinate address regions/peripheral windows behind axi_node_intf_wrap / permission-related RTL security issue
findings.reasoning_summary: Subject: AXI master/requester with privilege level Operation: read/write/route Object: AXI subordinate address regions/peripheral windows behind axi_node_intf_wrap Expected guard: routing to protected subordinates should enforce privilege/access matrix consistently for read and write channels using priv_lvl_i and/or AXI protection attributes; unprivileged accesses should not be routed or acknowledged as successful Observed behavior: connectivity_mapping sets connectivity_map_o[i][j] = access_ctrl_i[i][j][priv_lvl_i] || ((j==6) && access_ctrl_i[i][7][priv_lvl_i]). access_ctrl_i is generated in the harness from ROM2-derived access_ctrl_reg, and priv_lvl_i is sourced from the Ariane core priv_lvl_o. axi_node_intf_wrap maps start/end addresses for all peripheral windows and passes connectivity_map to axi_node. However, the provided excerpts do not include the full axi_node behavior, denial response behavior, burst-crossing behavior, or whether connectivity_map is applied consistently to AW and AR routing. Path: Ariane core/debug AXI masters -> axi_node_intf_wrap slave ports -> axi_node with start_addr_i/end_addr_i and connectivity map -> master[DRAM/GPIO/Ethernet/SPI/AES/UART/PLIC/CLINT/PKT/ROM2/ROM/Debug] Closure verdict: Related RTL evidence exists, but some core evidence slots remain incomplete.
findings.security_impact: If axi_node ignores or inconsistently applies connectivity_map, unprivileged masters may reach protected windows. Evidence shows an intended mechanism but not sufficient proof of correct enforcement.
findings.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: Need untruncated axi_node_intf_wrap connection to s_connectivity_map/s_start_addr/s_end_addr and axi_node implementation/denial response behavior.

findings.summary: APB windows for AES/PLIC/PKT/UART and AXI memory window for ROM2 are separately instantiated, but full address decode/psel/prdata mux behavior and alias-freedom across all windows cannot be proven from supplied excerpts.
findings.vulnerability_category: read/write/configure / PLIC, packet, AES, UART, ROM2 APB/AXI-visible register windows / permission-related RTL security issue
findings.reasoning_summary: Subject: software-visible APB/MMIO requester Operation: read/write/configure Object: PLIC, packet, AES, UART, ROM2 APB/AXI-visible register windows Expected guard: security-sensitive peripheral configuration registers should be reachable only through intended address windows and privilege policy; APB decode must not alias protected registers across peripherals or ignore psel/pwrite/penable Observed behavior: ariane_peripherals has distinct AXI input ports for plic, aes, rom2_fuse, pkt, uart, spi, and ethernet. PLIC, PKT, AES, and UART use separate axi2apb_64_32 bridges and apb_to_reg/APB peripherals; ROM2 uses a separate axi2mem bridge. AES APB path has its own aes_penable/pwrite/paddr/psel/prdata signals. Address window base/length values are provided to axi_node_intf_wrap in the testharness. However, the full psel generation, prdata muxing, and all peripheral address windows are not fully visible, and the upstream axi_node route policy is not fully proven. Path: AXI fabric address decode -> ariane_peripherals per-peripheral AXI port -> separate axi2apb_64_32 bridges for APB peripherals or axi2mem for ROM2 -> peripheral register interfaces Closure verdict: Related RTL evidence exists, but some core evidence slots remain incomplete.
findings.security_impact: If address ranges overlap or psel/prdata/write-enable logic aliases, software could reach sensitive AES/ROM2/PKT/PLIC registers through unintended windows. Current evidence does not prove such aliasing nor fully rule it out.
findings.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: Need full untruncated ariane_peripherals APB bridge blocks, prdata mux/default behavior, and axi_node decode behavior to confirm no overlapping range or simultaneous selection.

findings.summary: Debug ndmreset is driven from dm_top without visible authorization gating at the harness, and AES secret key input/intermediate read mux exposure can persist or reappear after reset because key material is supplied from ROM2 secure_reg and read access is unguarded.
findings.vulnerability_category: reset/mode transition / AES key/intermediate/control state, debug authorization state, ROM/peripheral security state / permission-related RTL security issue
findings.reasoning_summary: Subject: debug/JTAG reset requester or external reset source Operation: reset/mode transition Object: AES key/intermediate/control state, debug authorization state, ROM/peripheral security state Expected guard: debug-triggered reset should not leave security-sensitive state uncleared or force a mode transition that bypasses locks/authorization; reset sources should be authorized and clear key/intermediate/debug state as required Observed behavior: dm_top outputs ndmreset, which is combined with rst_ni in rstgen to produce ndmreset_n for core/fabric reset paths. No jtag_key/authentication guard is visible before ndmreset_o. aes_wrapper reset clears start, p_c, and state, but key_big is combinationally assigned from key_in and not locally cleared; key_in is driven from ROM2 key_reg_out[0] in ariane_peripherals. aes_wrapper read mux continues to expose key_big and inter_state slices without guard. ROM2 reloads secure_reg from const mem on reset, so key material is restored rather than scrubbed. Path: DMI/JTAG/debug request -> dm_top.ndmreset_o -> rstgen rst_ni & ~ndmreset -> ndmreset_n distributed to core/fabric; ROM2 reset reloads secure_reg; AES key_in/key_big and inter_state remain readable via AES MMIO read mux Closure verdict: All required core evidence slots are present for a candidate violation.
findings.security_impact: An unauthenticated debug reset can cause security-relevant mode transitions and does not scrub key material from the externally readable AES path. Post-reset software-visible reads may still disclose key material; debug reset authorization is not evidenced.
findings.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: Need aes_192_sed reset behavior for inter_state and full debug authentication policy to determine exact post-reset residue beyond key_in/key_big exposure.

global_uncertainty: No closure-level missing evidence was recorded beyond the visible source scope limitations.
